perturbation.original_text: 12. Governing Law. Except to the extent preempted by Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974, as amended ("ERISA"), all rights hereunder shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York without regard to its rules governing conflicts of laws, or the rules of any other jurisdiction which would cause the laws of any jurisdiction other than the State of New York to apply. If this Agreement is determined to be subject to ERISA, it is intended to be exempt from the reporting and disclosure provisions of ERISA pursuant to Section 104(a)(3) of ERISA and Department of Labor Regulation Section 2520.104-24.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. Governing Law. Except to the extent preempted by Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974, as amended ("ERISA"), all rights hereunder shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York with full regard to its rules governing conflicts of laws, or the rules of any other jurisdiction which would cause the laws of any jurisdiction other than the State of New York to apply. If this Agreement is determined to be subject to ERISA, it is intended to be exempt from all provisions of ERISA.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states the agreement is intended to be exempt from 'the reporting and disclosure provisions of ERISA'. The modified text states it is intended to be exempt from 'all provisions of ERISA'. This is a misaligned term because ERISA explicitly defines which agreements are subject to it. A private agreement cannot supersede federal law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 (ERISA)
perturbation.law_explanation: 29 U.S. Code § 1003 clearly defines the types of employee benefit plans that are subject to ERISA. The modified text attempts to exempt the agreement from 'all provisions of ERISA,' which contradicts the statute's explicit coverage rules. The contract cannot unilaterally decide it is not subject to ERISA if it meets the criteria for coverage under the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the agreement to be exempt from 'all provisions of ERISA,' while the original text states it is intended to be exempt from 'the reporting and disclosure provisions of ERISA'. 29 U.S. Code § 1003, as seen in Scraped Law Snippet 1, defines which employee benefit plans are subject to ERISA. The edited text attempts to exempt the agreement from all ERISA provisions, contradicting the law. A contract cannot unilaterally decide it is not subject to ERISA if it meets coverage criteria.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Company Default. In the event of a "Company Default" (as defined below), the Trust shall have the right to require the Company to cure the Company Default by notifying the Company in writing within sixty (60) days after its receipt of notice of a Company Default, or if later, within thirty (30) days after a Trustee becomes aware of the Company Default. If the Company fails to cure the Company Default within sixty (60) days after being notified by the Trust of the Company Default, the Trust shall have the right to require the Company to transfer its interest in the Policy to the Trust.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Company Default. In the event of a "Company Default" (as defined below), the Trust shall have the right to encourage the Company to cure the Company Default by notifying the Company in writing within sixty (60) days after its receipt of notice of a Company Default, or if later, within thirty (30) days after a Trustee becomes aware of the Company Default. If the Company fails to cure the Company Default within sixty (60) days after being notified by the Trust of the Company Default, the Trust shall have the right to request the Company to transfer its interest in the Policy to the Trust.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides the Trust with the 'right to require' the Company to take certain actions upon default. The modified text changes 'require' to 'encourage' and 'request', weakening the Trust's contractual rights and creating ambiguity in enforcement. This contradicts the legally binding nature of contracts, which are meant to establish clear obligations, particularly under New York contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Contract Law - Enforceability of Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: N.Y. U.C.C. § 1-304 implies a duty of good faith in the performance of contracts. By weakening the Trust's ability to enforce the contract upon Company Default from a 'right to require' to a 'right to encourage/request', the modification introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as a lack of good faith, especially if the Company refuses to comply. This contradiction undermines the enforceability and the intended binding nature of the contractual agreement under New York law.

Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within this act imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the Trust's rights, changing 'require' to 'encourage' and 'request'. This undermines the enforceability of the contract, as the company is no longer strictly obligated to comply with the Trust's demands upon default. N.Y. U.C.C. § 1-304, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, imposes an 'obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement' for every contract. By making compliance optional, the modification could be seen as a failure to act in good faith, because the modified text allows the company to simply ignore the Trust's demand, which contradicts the intent of the original agreement and the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Payment of Premiums. Subject to the terms of this Agreement, the Company agrees to pay premiums on the Policy as provided under the Policy. In the event this Agreement is terminated in accordance with terms of Section 11, the Company shall not be liable for any premiums owed on the Policy after the date of termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Payment of Premiums. Subject to the terms of this Agreement, the Company intends to pay premiums on the Policy as provided under the Policy. In the event this Agreement is terminated in accordance with terms of Section 11, the Company may still be liable for any premiums owed on the Policy after the date of termination.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to pay' to 'intends to pay' weakens the obligation. Stating the Company 'may still be liable' when previously it 'shall not be liable' introduces ambiguity and creates uncertainty. This contradicts the foundational principle of contractual certainty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Contract Law - Offer and Acceptance
perturbation.law_explanation: Under New York contract law, an agreement requires a clear offer and acceptance, resulting in a binding commitment. The modified text replaces 'agrees' with 'intends', indicating a lack of a firm commitment regarding premium payments. Similarly, switching from 'shall not be liable' to 'may still be liable' after termination introduces ambiguity, contradicting the principle of a definite agreement. This undermines the validity of the contractual obligation by creating uncertainty regarding the company's commitment to pay premiums as per the contract terms.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'agrees to pay' to 'intends to pay' weakens the commitment to payment, and 'shall not be liable' to 'may still be liable' introduces ambiguity regarding liability after termination. This contradicts the principle of contractual certainty, which requires a clear and binding agreement. While the provided snippets do not explicitly define contractual certainty, the Law Explanation indicates that under New York contract law, an agreement requires a clear offer and acceptance, resulting in a binding commitment, something undermined by the modified text.
